coredata.dc:title: Optical properties and zeta potential of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) dispersed nematic liquid crystal 4′- heptyl-4-biphenylcarbonitrile (7CB)
coredata.dc:creator: Pandey, Fanindra Pati
Rastogi, Ayushi
Singh, Shri
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDS) of three concentrations 0.1, 0.2 and 0.5 wt% have been dispersed in nematic liquid crystal 4′-Heptyl-4-biphenylcarbonitrile (7 CB). The results on the optical properties and zeta potential of pure and CQDs dispersed nematic 7CB have been reported. It has been observed that the dispersion of CQDs in host nematic matrix induces quenching of photoluminescence and reduces the Ultra-Violet-Visible (UV–Vis) absorbance of pure nematic at a lower wavelength. From the result of fluorescence decay profile, the fluorescence life time of pristine and dispersed nematic systems have been obtained. No remarkable changes in nematic lifetime in the presence of CQDs have been observed, which confirm that no energy is transferred from nematic to CQDs. Further, it has been found that due to dispersion of CQDs the numerical values of zeta potential are increased for the dispersed systems with 0.1 and 0.2 wt% concentrations of CQDs as compared to pure 7 CB. This result demonstrates that the study of zeta potential provides useful information on the stability and aggregation of the CQDs dispersed nematic systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nematic liquid crystal
Carbon quantum dots
Time resolved fluorescence
UV–Vis absorbance
Zeta potential

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: F .P. Pandey and A. Rastogi are thankful to UGC , New Delhi, for the financial support. We also acknowledge Dr. A. S. Parmar, Biophysics and Nanotechnology Laboratory, Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Banaras Hindu University for providing CQDs and for providing necessary experimental facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the FS 920 Steady- State spectrometer configuration.
FL 920 Fluorescence life time Spectrometer Configuration.
(a) TEM images of CQDs at 100 nm resolution (b) Electron diffraction of (SAED) pattern of CQDs (c) Size distribution of CQDs [45].
UV–Vis spectra of pure and CQD Dispersed nematic 7CB.
Photoluminescence spectra of pure 7CB and CQD Dispersed 7CB.
Variation of full width half maxima (FWHM) and peak intensity of fluorescence for pure and 0.1, 0.2 and 0.5 wt% CQDs dispersed nematic 7CB systems.
Variation of I0/I with the change of dopant concentration.
Fluorescence decay profile for pure and CQDs dispersed nematic 7CB systems.
Result of mean zeta potential, Electrophoretic Mobility, and Conductivity of CQDs, pure LC and CQD dispersed systems.
Full width half maxima values of various peaks obtained in deconvoluted spectra of pure 7 CB and its dispersed system with CQDs (0.1 wt%, 0.2 wt% and 0.5 wt% concentration of CQDs in 7 CB).
Parameters obtained from lifetime measurement of pure 7CB and its dispersed systems with CQDs.

head.ce:title: Optical properties and zeta potential of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) dispersed nematic liquid crystal 4′- heptyl-4-biphenylcarbonitrile (7CB)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi, 221 005, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi, 221 005, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Lucknow University, Lucknow, 226007, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Lucknow University, Lucknow, 226007, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDS) of three concentrations 0.1, 0.2 and 0.5 wt% have been dispersed in nematic liquid crystal 4′-Heptyl-4-biphenylcarbonitrile (7 CB). The results on the optical properties and zeta potential of pure and CQDs dispersed nematic 7CB have been reported. It has been observed that the dispersion of CQDs in host nematic matrix induces quenching of photoluminescence and reduces the Ultra-Violet-Visible (UV–Vis) absorbance of pure nematic at a lower wavelength. From the result of fluorescence decay profile, the fluorescence life time of pristine and dispersed nematic systems have been obtained. No remarkable changes in nematic lifetime in the presence of CQDs have been observed, which confirm that no energy is transferred from nematic to CQDs. Further, it has been found that due to dispersion of CQDs the numerical values of zeta potential are increased for the dispersed systems with 0.1 and 0.2 wt% concentrations of CQDs as compared to pure 7 CB. This result demonstrates that the study of zeta potential provides useful information on the stability and aggregation of the CQDs dispersed nematic systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Liquid crystalline materials exhibit interesting dielectric, electro-optical and spectroscopic properties because of their large anisotropy coupled with the collective reorientation of molecules. The investigation of these properties is important from the point of view of basic understanding and applied applications. From the application point of view, the LC materials must possess certain characteristic qualities. For achieving these qualities and removing disadvantages, in recent decades, the interest and emphasis on the science and technology of LC materials have shifted towards two main directions: (i) to synthesize new LC materials with specific properties, and (ii) to make composite systems and modify their properties by the dispersion/doping of non-mesogenic materials (nanoparticles, quantum dots, nanorods, carbon nanotubes, dyes, polymers, etc) into the host LC matrix so that the dispersed system becomes suitable for the applications [1–4]. In recent years, the subject of quantum dots (QDs) or nanoparticles (NPs) dispersed liquid crystals has emerged as a new frontier area of research [1–41]. The main emphasis of research in this area is to develop understandings about (a) how the characteristics and properties of LC phases are influence/modified in dispersed materials and (b) the self-organizing properties in LCs to assemble, manipulate, and provide template synthesis of nanomaterials.

Three different magic-sized CdSe QDs with 1–5 wt% concentrations were doped in nematic LC 5-n-heptyl-2-(4-n-octyloxyphenyl)-pyrimidine [6]. The influence of QDs doping on the optical, alignment and electro-optic properties of nematic was investigated. It was shown that although, the electro-optical properties of dispersed system is marginally altered but the director field in the birefringent strips, homeotropic alignment in the surrounding domains and very significant aggregation of QD were observed. The effects of size, capping agent, and concentration of CdSe and CdTe QDs doped into a nematic Felix-2900-03 on the optical and electro-optical properties of composite system were investigated [5] and found that all the QDs were effective in reducing the threshold voltage and splat elastic constant. Mirzaei et al. [10] have nicely addressed the question how the addition of CdSe and CdTe QDs as additive in host nematic LC affect the optical, electro-optical and alignment properties of mixture and have inferred that exploration of LC motif-capped QDs is a valuable strategy to develop a LC-QDs composite suitable for the applications.

The effects of dispersed CdSe/ZnS QDs on the electrical, optical, fluorescent correlation, NI phase transition driven organized assemblies of QDs, dynamics and relaxation optical response, and reversible capacitance change characteristics of nematic LCs have been studied by several groups [8,12,14,18]. Konshina et al. [8] have shown that due to dispersion of QDs into nematic LC based on cyanobiphenyl the threshold voltage of the electrooptical splay effect and phase delay of light and effective dielectric permittivity are decreased. Prodanov et al. [12] have found that CdSe/ZnS QDs−nematic LC colloids was thermodynamically stable for at least one year, and the colloids remained stable due to the minimization of the distortion of the LC ordering around the QDs. The NI phase transition was used to create organized assemblies of CdSe/ZnS core/shell QDs by Rodarte et al. [14]. They also showed that the location of the assemblies was controllable by utilizing beads as defect nucleation points. Konshina et al. [18] observed variation of the LC cell capacitance with the CdSe/ZnS QDs concentration of about 0.18 wt%. In an another work, these authors [17] also observed that the optical response time and threshold voltage are reduced with the increase of QDs concentration in the range of 0.5–1.5 mg/ml in nematic LC. As compared to pure nematic the threshold voltage of QDs dispersed system was reduced twice with the concentration of QDs. Based on elastic continuum theory model which considered the interaction of nematic molecules with the surrounding molecules, QDs and electric field, Petrescu et al. [33] studied theoretically the dynamic behavior of a mixture consisting of nematic 5CB and CdSe/ZnS QDs and found an improvement in the turn-off time of the mixture. Cd10xZnxS/ZnS core cell QDs were dispersed in nematic LCs BBHA by Tripathi et al. [29] and in NLC2020 by Rastogi et al. [31]. The farmer authors analyzed the impact of QDs dispersion, applied bias and QDs concentration on the dielectric permittivity, dielectric loss, response time, transmittance and birefringence as a function of frequency and temperature for the planar alignment, while Rastogi et al. characterized the dielectric and electro-optical parameters for both the planar and homeotropic alignments. It has been concluded by both the groups that the addition of QDs into nematic LCs suggest an efficient way to alter the dielectric and electyro-optical properties of nematic LCs, making them suitable for the applications in LC based devices. Similar Conclusions were drawn by many other groups by dispersing in different nematic LCs the CIS–ZnS- [13], ZnO- [16], graphene- [19], colloidal- [20], lnP/ZnS- [32], and CdS [39] QDs.

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have caught attention due to their exceptional electronic and optical properties with tunable band gap, excitation dependent emission, high fluorescent quantum yields, upconversion, low toxicity, chemical inertness, low cost of production, water solubility, biocompatibility, etc. [42–46]. Very recently, Shukla et al. [45] have synthesized CQDs using a green eco-friendly and medicinal Ocimum tenuiflorum (or Tulsi) plant via hydrothermal method in a facile one-step approach. In this work, we have dispersed this CQDs of three concentrations (0.1, 0.2 and 0.5 wt%) into nematic LC 7CB and have studied the optical properties and zeta potential of pristine and CQDs dispersed nematic systems. The manuscript is organized as follows: The experimental method and morphology of CQDs are discussed in sec 2, while sec. 3 is devoted to the results and discussions. Paper ends with the conclusions in last sec. 4.

The nematic LC material 7CB (4′- Heptyl-4-biphenylcarbonitrile) has been purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been used as synthesized by Shukla et al. [45]. The molecular length of 7CB is 18.7 Å and it shows the phase sequence: Cr− 30−N−42.8−IL; transition temperatures are in 0C. The CQDs of three concentrations 0.1, 0.2 and 0.5 wt% have been dispersed in nematic 7CB and sample is ultrasonicated for 1 h in ultrahigh ultrasonic bath of 6.5 L for making homogeneous mixture. All the experiments have been performed in DI water in solution form.

The morphology and characteristic features of CQDs have been studied by Shukla et al. [45]. These are of quasi spherical shape having average size between 1–3 nm as revealed by TEM analysis and the SAED pattern (Fig. 3 ) that shows the diffused concentric rings suggesting the presence of amorphous carbon.

Zeta-potential is a measure of degree of electrostatic repulsion between adjacent similar charged particle in dispersed system. Table 1 lists the values of mean zeta-potential, electrophoretic mobility, conductivity and standard deviation for pure and CQDs dispersed nematic 7 CB systems. We have studied the dispersed systems having three concentrations 0.1, 0.2 and 0.5 wt% of CQDs in 7 CB. Using Anton Paar (Litesizer) 500 ™ instrument, we have measured the zeta-potential, electrophoretic mobility and conductivity of pure and CQDs dispersed systems. Table 1 shows the variation of zeta-potential distribution profile with the concentration of CQDs dispersion in 7 CB. It can be seen from Table 1 that the absolute value of zeta-potential is minimum for pristine 7 CB and maximum for 0.2 wt% CQDs dispersed system. It is obvious that zeta potential for all the dispersed systems is greater than pure 7 CB and CQDs. The negative value of zeta potential signifies that the electrostatic forces are repulsive between the molecules. Larger is the negative charge greater is the repulsive force between the molecules. Thus the dispersed systems will be more stable than the pure system. Also the 0.2 wt% CQDs dispersed system will be more stable than its counterparts with the 0.1 and 0.5 wt% dispersed systems. The minimum stability of pure 7 CB and 0.5 wt% concentration can be attributed to the incipient attractive forces.

The concentration dependences of electrophoretic mobility and conductivity of the pure and CQDs dispersed 7 CB systems are shown in Table 1. It can be seen that the electrophoretic mobility and conductivity is maximum for the 0.2 wt% dispersion of CQDs in host nematic. In comparison to pristine 7 CB the values of both the electrophoretic mobility and conductivity increase rapidly by the dispersion of CQDs in nematic. The behavior of zeta potential, electrophoretic mobility and conductivity for pure and CQDs dispersed nematic 7 CB systems can be attributed to the LC- LC, LC- CQDs, CQDs- CQDs and other electrostatic and dispersion interactions. For small 0.1 wt% concentration of CQDs the interaction between LC- CQDs plays the dominant role. It is clear that net surface charge on CQD and LC molecules is –ve which is depicted from -ve zeta values, therefore CQDs in LC medium (which is a mid polar solvent for CQDs) experiences electrostatic repulsive forces between LC and CQDs molecules and forms stable suspension. For 0.2 wt% which contains intermediate concentrations of CQDs in LCs, LC-CQD as well as CQDs- CQDs interactions has the dominant role with strong electrostatic repulsive forces. However, high values of zeta potential for 0.1 and 0.2 wt% suggest the existence of other electrokinetic interactions such as DEP (dielectrophoretic forces) along with EP (Electrophoretic) forces that form the complicated composite structure [47]. There is also existence of steric hindrance in the composite structure because of presence of steric bulk in the mixtures. This might be the possible reason for the difference in the values of electrophoretic mobility and conductivity of 0.1 and 0.2 wt%. For 0.5 wt% concentration of CQDs, cluster of CQDs are formed and dipolar interaction becomes dominant leading to decrease in momentum originating from these cluster of CQDs which diminishes the charge transfer between CQDs and nematics. Hence the decrease of zeta potential values results in aggregation of particles in 0.5 wt% with reduced mobility and conductivity values of 0.5 wt% CQDs dispersed system.

Fig. 4 shows the variation of Normalized absorbance with wavelength (nm) for pure and QDs dispersed in 7 CB systems 34 °C. For both pristine and dispersed systems two peaks, a smaller one at 220 nm and another pronounced peak at 287 nm occur. In between these peaks dip in absorbance at 245 nm for all the systems is found. Fig. 4 also shows how the characteristic features, peaks and dip positions vary with the concentration of CQDs in host nematics. The dip shows absorption loss in nematic liquid crystal. This may be attributed to the dependence of absorption and scattering losses on wavelength. The absorption tail of nematic varies with wavelength as 1/λ2 and scattering as 1/λ4. From the de Gennes theory [48], light scattering caused by the orientational fluctuation of nematic molecules is very strong. So it may dominate optical transmission losses leading to dip in the absorption. Both the light scattering and the absorption in the diluted solution are decreased at approximately same proportion. This happens due to low nematic concentration in the solution (0.5 wt %). However, in UV region the resonant absorption due to n-pi* transition is greater (287 nm). The absorbance of pristine CQD at 35 °C is already reported in Ref. [45]. It can be seen from Fig. 4 that pure and CQDs dispersed nematic 7CB absorb a broad regime of UV light between 200 and 400 nms. In case of dispersion of 0.5 wt% of CQDs in 7CB, the interparticle interaction between CQDs is increased due to their close proximity with the available volume. Because of this close proximity, it is expected that agglomerative nature of particles should cause increase in absorbance. This variation in absorption is only indicative of the change in dispersion stability after the addition of CQDs in pure 7CB. Due to UV absorbance the dispersed system becomes suitable for the UV light storage applications.

The light scattering mechanism for the nematic and isotropic states is different. In the nematic phase the light scattering is caused by the long range orientational fluctuation of LC director. On the other hand, the light scattering can be induced by short range orientational fluctuation and small density fluctuation in isotropic state. However, scattering is lower in isotropic state as compared to nematic phase. Therefore, it is expected that in isotropic phase the light scattering is less than absorption.

A Photoluminescence spectrum of pure CQD has already been reported by Shukla et al. [45]. The PL quenching can be induced via the destruction of emission groups. The chemical structure of CQDs can be destroyed due to hydroxyl group having ultrahigh oxidation potential. This can lead to PL centre destruction and PL quenching. Usually, the PL of CQDs can originate due to radiative recombination of excitons in carbon core. So we have assumed that CQDs are present in solution as cross linked molecular clusters but not as solid nanoparticles. The structures result through the dehydration and incomplete carbonization of carboxyl, hydroxyl and amine groups during hydrothermal process. There remains no doubt that PL is associated with the clusters. We conjectured that the PL mechanism is because of molecular states in CQDs. The PL mainly arose from integral molecules and chemical groups connected to soft clusters. Protonation and deprotonation of molecule affect HOMO and LUMO of fluorescent molecule. Furthermore, we think that two or more different PL states may exist in CQDs [49]. The most important state was the molecular state and PL peak in CQD appears at 405 nm on excitation at 320 nm as already reported [45]. Fig. 5 shows PL spectra of 7CB at 34 °C in pure and CQDs dispersed systems. The spectra consist of broad band (with weak peaks at 376 nm and 382 nm) of pure 7CB emission, while the shape of PL spectra remains unchanged. These peaks may result from other PL centers: carbon clusters and surface states (due to functionalization of many electron accepting nitrogen and oxygen rich groups). It has been noticed that significant quenching occurs in 0.5 wt% QDs dispersed system as compared to pure 7CB. The 0.1 and 0.2 wt% QDs dispersed systems exhibit suppressed quenching than 0.5 wt%. Both the molecular and carbon cluster states of CQDs can be easily quenched by the hydroxyl radicals.

Less efficient charge transfer occurs from the near surface LC molecules to CQDs due to their electron donating and electron accepting surface functionalized groups. This leads to efficient quenching of the luminescence in 7CB/CQDs system. Based on the luminescence of excimers, the nematic phase of pristine 7CB can be characterized. The molecular alignment promotes their formation. We conclude that spectra obtained for pure 7CB and its dispersed system with CQDs corresponds to excimer emission by 7CB molecules. The dipole moment of 7CB molecules leads to strong dipole interaction, conformational change, and dimer formation. Therefore bent conformation of nematic molecule is the characteristics of monomers of liquid crystals and their presence in the sample indicates partial disruption of nematic alignment of 7CB by CQD particles [49].

The primary fluorescence emission peak of pure 7 CB has been found to be at 376 nm and it does not change with the concentration of CQDs in 7CB except the change in FWHM values as shown in Table 2 . It has been observed that the secondary fluorescence intensity has been stimulated by the addition of three concentrations of CQDs in 7CB. This is indicated from the asymmetric broadening in the fluorescence emission. The stimulation of secondary emission is dependent on the dopant concentrations.

Fig. 6
shows the deconvoluted fluorescence spectra for Pure and CQDs dispersed nematic 7CB systems. It can be seen that there are three emission peaks (P1 to P3) for pure and dispersed systems with 0.1 and 0.2 wt% concentrations of QDs and 4 emission peaks (P1 to P4) corresponding to 0.5 wt% concentration. The emission peaks P1, P2, P3 referred to pure and 0.1, 0.2 wt% concentrations of CQDs correspond to FWHM >25 nm. However, in case of 0.5 wt% concentration of CQDs, P1 and P2 correspond to FWHM <25 nm which has been attributed to band edge emissions and for P3, P4, FWHM >25 nm which gives the evidence of surface state emissions (see Fig. 7).

All deconvoluted peaks for pure 7CB, 0.1 to 0.5 wt% are superimposed to produce broad fluorescence emissions as shown in Fig. 5. These changes are associated with accumulation of charges at the interface of CQD and 7CB molecules. It is clear from the values of zeta potential that the CQD has n-type conductivity and pure 7CB has ionic impurity. Therefore, interference in the fluorescence emission occurs due to the accumulation of charge carrier at the interface of CQDs- 7CB molecules during the charge transfer phenomena [27]. These results indicate that CQDs behave as a quencher for 7CB molecules. The differentiation in the band edge and surface state emissions are based on the FWHM values. For FWHM ≤25 nm emissions are analogous to band edge whereas surface state emissions have wide spectral band width >25 nm. In time resolve fluorescence spectra of FLC-QDs composite system, Singh et al. [27] have observed the absence of FRET. The comparative variation in the band edge and surface state emission for Cd based nanomaterials has been reported by Bae et al. [39]. In the present work, we have did not find the carrier multiplication and other recombination processes because there is no change in time resolved photoluminescence.

As reported by Singh et al. [27], FRET mechanisms is influenced by the proximity distance of CQDs to 7CB molecules which in our case has been observed to be 90 nm, 88 nm, 96 nm and 100 nm for pure, and 0.1, 0.2 and 0.5 wt% CQDs dispersed nematic 7CB systems. This provides the evidence for the absence of FRET in our study. It also depends on the dipolar interactions, acceptor-donor concentrations, variation and orientation of donor and acceptor molecules.

The analysis of quenching mechanism is performed by the plot of I0/I versus concentration of CQDs as shown in Fig. 8 . It shows the deviation of linear behavior because of the existence of static and dynamic quenching phenomena. From Fig. 8, it is clear that static quenching behavior is observed at low concentration 0 ≤ 0.1 wt% ≤ 0.2 wt% where as the non linear behavior has been noticed at higher ≥0.5 wt% concentrations of CQDs in 7CB.The possible reason for the behavior might be the interparticle interactions between CQD which gets involved in linear quenching. I0/I versus concentrations of CQDs obey the Stern-volmer quenching equation [26]:
(1)
I 0
I
=
{
1 +
K
s v
( Q )
e
α
( Q )
}
Here I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of the nanoparticles. Ksv is Stern-volmer quenching constant and α is the fitting parameter. The static and dynamic quenching behavior depends on the static and dynamic behavior, size, composition and shape of the dopant.

Fig. 9 shows the fluorescence decay profile for pure and CQDs dispersed nematic systems. UV light irradiation induces decrease in PL lifetime which can be attributed to partial destruction of molecular state of PL centers. The equilibrium is achieved after several minutes because the PL quenching is an irreversible chemical reaction. In case of other quenchers the PL quenching occurs as soon as they are added. With the increase of temperature the number of OH radicals the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs increases. Therefore, at high temperature the quenching effect was enhanced. It has been observed that fluorescence lifetime of CQDs is lower than that of pure 7CB.

It has been observed that pure CQDs exhibit short nanosecond lifetime and multi-exponential emission decay. This decay occurs due to different discrete states, localized electrons in conduction band in shallow trapped states, rapid band gap transitions and emission related to oxygen containing functional groups. Short nanoseconds lifetime is indicative of the fast radiative recombination of excitons species [45]. One of the most noticeable properties of CQDs is their intrinsic PL. This causes excitation dependent fluorescence with maximum intensity at excitation wavelength.

When the CQDs are dispersed in pure nematic 7CB, it produces quenched PL spectra due to disruption of PL centers as explained earlier in sec 3.2. Thus, the fluorescence lifetime for CQDs dispersed system increases as compared to 7CB and pure CQD as shown in Table 3 . The decay in fluorescence lifetime of pure 7CB and its mixtures with CQD indicates non-radiative recombination process that comes into existence which was suppressed in pure CQD and so the luminescence quenching occurs.

Two different emission peaks 376 nm and 382 nm had been observed when excited by 285 nm for all samples. Absorption is an important parameter which impacts the photo stability and life-time of LC based devices. Very crucial information on the electronic transitions associated with the absorption curve can be elucidated. Fig. 4 shows the variations of normalized absorption spectra with wavelength of pure and dispersed systems. The peaks of this curve correspond to the photoluminescence (PL) emission.

The understanding about the origin of fluorescence can be generated from the time-resolved fluorescence data. The time resolved fluorescence provides way for the absolute measure of surface energy transfer. It is independent of concentration and fluctuations in the excitation source intensity. The fluorescence decay at 376 nm and 382 nm is shown in Fig. 5. The nematic/CQDs composite has been excited by 283 nm. Our motivation was to excite the CQD (n-p*) transition slowly so that its emission can be utilized as excitation for the nematic material. The nature of all decay curves is apparently non-mono-exponential. This indicates the coexistence of multiple emitting species. We have fitted the time resolved data with a tri-exponential function,
(2)
< t >
=
(
t 1
A 1
+
t 2
A 2
+
t 3
A 3
)
A 1
+
A 2
+
A 3
where t1, t2 and t3 are the short and long lifetime components, and A1, A2 and A3 are, respectively, the normalized amplitude components. We have judged the acceptability of the fits by using reduced R2 square test. The residual of the functions was fitted to the actual data. In Fig. 5 we have seen a shortening of PL decay time of CQDs. This confirms the non-radiative energy transfer.

When the decay component in CQDs is shorter, it is an indication of the emission due to the radiative relaxation of excited electrons making transition to ground state. However, the origin of longer decay component is still relatively uncertain. The longer decay component existing in the PL may be due to the radiative decay occurring from the electron–hole recombination on the surface containing localized states [20–23]. For different LCs, the variation in the rate of non-radiative decay is caused by the variation in the type of quenching centers and their numbers. This is responsible for a distribution of decay times. For fitted parameter R2 is listed in Table 3 for all the samples. The efficient energy transfer leads to reduction in lifetime in the donor–acceptor system. However, it has been observed that the lifetimes of the both pure and dispersed system are not significantly decreased due to addition of CQDs. This confirms the absence of energy transfer from LCs to CQDs. The time resolved data reveals that LCs exhibit tri-exponential decays with three lifetimes. Further, the higher lifetime component t3 can be attributed to the carrier recombination process. The decay time constant (t1, t2, t3), their fraction contribution, and the amplitudes (A1, A2, A3) depend on the quality of LCs surface.

In brevity, CQDs were dispersed in nematic material in different concentrations. In order to obtain information about the interactions between LC and CQD molecules, we have investigated the steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy including UV spectroscopy. The quenching of PL intensity has been observed in pure 7CB with the addition of CQDs. The quenching nature follows Stern-Volmer equation. Steady state and time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy have been carried out in the present work. The life time of the composite system has been found to be almost similar with pure 7CB. The proximity distance of CQDs is observed to be > 10 nm which depicts the absence of FRET. The change in UV–Vis absorbance, Fluorescence and life time are attributed to the molecular interaction between 7CB and CQDs. The dispersed system with 0.5 wt% CQDs shows aggregation effect. This aggregation is evident from zeta values which have been found to be least for this particular concentration. This composite exhibits minimum electrostatic force of repulsion between CQDs particles that leads to accumulation whereas the high value of zeta potential indicates high repulsive interaction between similar charge particles that stabilizes the 7CB and CQDs composite systems. The fluorescence quenching phenomena is widely applicable to obtain useful information about the structure and dynamics of biological macro -molecular systems.
Fanindra Pati Pandey: Formal analysis. Ayushi Rastogi: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Shri Singh: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: F .P. Pandey and A. Rastogi are thankful to UGC, New Delhi, for the financial support. We also acknowledge Dr. A. S. Parmar, Biophysics and Nanotechnology Laboratory, Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Banaras Hindu University for providing CQDs and for providing necessary experimental facilities.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Emissivity and electro-optical properties of semiconducting quantum dots/rods and liquid crystal composites: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Singh, M. Fisch, S. Kumar, Emissivity and electro-optical properties of semiconducting quantum dots/rods and liquid crystal composites: a review, Rep. Prog. Phys. 79 (2016) 056502-056548.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanotube dispersion in nematic liquid crystals: an overview

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. P. Yadav, S. Singh, Carbon nanotube dispersion in nematic liquid crystals: an overview, Prog. mater. Sci. 80 (2016) 38−76.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Impact of dispersion of nanoscale particles on the properties of nematic liquid crystals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Singh, Impact of dispersion of nanoscale particles on the properties of nematic liquid crystals, Crystals 9 (2019) 475-1-17.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ferroelectric liquid crystal nanocomposites: recent development and future perspective

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. P. Yadav, K. yadav, J. Lahiri, A. S. Parmar, Ferroelectric liquid crystal nanocomposites: recent development and future perspective, Liq. Cryst. Reviews 6 (2019) 143−169.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of size, capping agent and concentration of CdSe and CdTe quantum dots doped into nematic liquid crystal on the optical and electro-optical properties of the final colloidal liquid crystal mixture

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Kinkead, T. Hegmann, Effect of size, capping agent and concentration of CdSe and CdTe quantum dots doped into nematic liquid crystal on the optical and electro-optical properties of the final colloidal liquid crystal mixture, J. Mater. Chem. 20 (2010) 448-458.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanocomposites of a nematic liquid crystal doped with magic sized CdSe quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Mirzaei, M. Urbanski, K. Yu, S. H. Kitzerow, T. Hegmann, Nanocomposites of a nematic liquid crystal doped with magic sized CdSe quantum dots, J. Mater. Chem. 21 (2011) 12710-12716.
